SUMMARY INFORMATION.Application Title: NIHR Development and Skills Enhancement Award Cohort 5

Co-Applicants.Organisation: Cheshire and Merseyside Specialist Perinatal Service
Co-Applicants.Position: Ruth O’Shaughnessy is a Consultant Clinical Psychologist and Co-Clinical Lead for the award-winning Cheshire and Merseyside Specialist Perinatal Service and for the North West Coast Perinatal Clinical Network. She has published numerous academic papers and book chapters in the field of attachment, perinatal, and parental mental illness. Importantly, she has substantial clinical and academic experience in the field of perinatal mental health inequalities, barriers to service access and treatment uptake as well as parenting across cultures, e.g. she co- authored the service-wide guidance on opening up conversations about race, ethnicity and culture. She also has extensive experience of supervising early to mid-career professionals. During the placement, Dr O’Shaughnessy will provide frequent one-to-one supervision meetings focussing on clinical and equalities aspects of the advanced fellowship proposal. Additionally, Dr O’Shaughnessy is ideally placed to introduce me to a wide range of professionals with extensive experience of culturally-sensitive and trauma-informed implementation of evidence-based therapies in NHS PMHS. The various research group meetings she chairs, or is involved in, will form great opportunities for gaining feedback on the fellowship proposal.

Co-Applicants.Organisation: University College London & Anna Freud Centre
Co-Applicants.Position: Professor Rachel Hiller leads the Child Trauma and Recovery Research Group at UCL as a professor of Child & Adolescent Mental Health and is the head of postgraduate studies at the Anna Freud Centre. Professor Hilller’s research focusses on the identification of key psychological and social processes linking trauma and maltreatment experiences to mental health. Additionally, she has expertise on the development and testing of scalable mental health intervention for trauma-exposed young people and the implementation within and across social care and mental health services. Importantly, Professor Hiller is renowned for co-developing mixed-methods research across sectors that have typically not been represented in research. Currently, professor Hiller leads multiple trials, including the MRC-funded ADaPT trial and the NIHR-ARC-funded ReThink project. Importantly, she has extensive experience in the supervision and mentoring of early career researchers. During the placement, Professor Hiller will provide frequent one-to-one supervision meetings focussing on applied, mixed methods, cross-sector research in the field of complex trauma. The various research group meetings she chairs, or is involved in, will form great opportunities for gaining feedback on the fellowship proposal. Additionally, access to the participation teams within Anna Freud will provide a valuable resource for co-development of future Reference: NIHR306150 Date submitted: 21/11/2024 Page 10 of 58 Development and Skills Enhancement Cohort 5 projects.

Professor Pariante is Professor of Biological Psychiatry at the Institute of Psychiatry, Psychology and Neuroscience, KCL, and Consultant Perinatal Psychiatrist at the South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trust. He has received multiple awards, including the 2017 Andrea Leadsom Award for Outstanding Contribution to the Field of Infant Mental Health. Professor Pariante’s research focusses on the role of stress and inflammation in the development of mental disorders and has a particular interest in the perinatal period. Currently, Professor Pariante works on multiple projects, including the NIHR-funded Maudsley Biomedical Research Centre; the MRC- funded CELEBRATE; and the Technology Strategy Board-funded HappyMums: understanding, predicting, and treating depression in pregnancy to improve mothers and offspring mental health outcomes. During the placement, Professor Pariante will provide frequent one-to-one supervision aiming to discuss clinical and neurobiological aspects of the advanced fellowship proposal. In addition, professor Pariante’s expertise in accessible dissemination of mental health research will aid in the development of future dissemination plans through an in-depth introduction to his award-winning online magazine.

SUMMARY BUDGET:
Lead Applicant Salary Cost £111544
Training and Development Cost £8445
Travel and Subsistence Cost £1500
Justification of Summary Budget
The lead applicant has worked with University College London (UCL) as a post-doctoral research fellow for
a number of years now. Given her strong track record of impactful academic publications, securing funding and
overall academic success, a starting point of the lower band of a senior research fellow is deemed appropriate by
the Head of the Division of Psychology and Language Sciences at UCL.
The £3000 super visions costs are included within the 'Training and Development Cost'.
Total Summary Budget Requested
£121,489.00

given if your application is successful

in the proposal.

at the host organisation.
In this section only include funding requested from NIHR, any matched funding should be detailed in the host's
supporting statement.

Contextual Factors
outputs from your research career to date. NIHR requests information on such circumstances so that the
Committees may take them into consideration during the assessment of your application. Contextual factors could
include:
 Career breaks due to parental leave, or periods of illness.
 Reduced time spent undertaking research activities (including research outputs), due to disability or to
caring responsibilities. This may include physical or mental health-related impairments or conditions, which
have impacted your opportunities to undertake research activities
 Reduced opportunities for career support e.g. mentorship, and limited opportunities to undertake prior
research and training.
 Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on your research career
Please also use this section to detail any other factors that may have impacted your research career not listed in
the examples provided. The impact on your career to date will be specific to your particular circumstances but
could include such impacts as limited opportunities to obtain grant funding or fewer publications. In general terms,
contextual factors should be significant, and relevant.
NIHR acknowledges that you may be reluctant, or uncomfortable disclosing relevant information that is sensitive.
However, you should bear in mind that we are unable to take into account factors that you do not disclose. Please
be assured that the information provided by you is sensitive and will be treated confidentially and in line with the
General Data and Protection Regulations (GDPR).
Completion of the contextual factors section of the application form does not automatically comprise a request for
reasonable adjustments, and if adjustments are required we recommend that applicants reach out to the relevant
programme team directly.
There have been several contextual factors affecting my academic career and I appreciate the Selection
Committee taking these into account. Since starting my PhD in 2016, I have given birth to my two
wonderful children and have taken maternity leave which has caused some delays in completion of my PhD. Our
family then moved to the UK just before the pandemic. During the pandemic, nursery closures and home schooling
has put considerable constraints on dedicated time for research activities such as writing up funding application
and publications. In addition, I have myself experience serious mental health difficulties after the birth of my
second child and was forced to take some time off work to recover.

research_support.text:
Ruth O’Shaughnessy is a Consultant Clinical Psychologist and Co-Clinical Lead for the award-winning Cheshire
and Merseyside Specialist Perinatal Service and for the North West Coast Perinatal Clinical Network. She has
published numerous academic papers and book chapters in the field of attachment, perinatal, and parental mental
illness. Importantly, she has substantial clinical and academic experience in the field of perinatal mental health
inequalities, barriers to service access and treatment uptake as well as parenting across cultures, e.g. she co-
authored the service-wide guidance on opening up conversations about race, ethnicity and culture. She also has
extensive experience of supervising early to mid-career professionals.
During the placement, Dr O’Shaughnessy will provide frequent one-to-one supervision meetings focussing on
clinical and equalities aspects of the advanced fellowship proposal. Additionally, Dr O’Shaughnessy is ideally
placed to introduce me to a wide range of professionals with extensive experience of culturally-sensitive and
trauma-informed implementation of evidence-based therapies in NHS PMHS. The various research group
meetings she chairs, or is involved in, will form great opportunities for gaining feedback on the fellowship proposal.
/ EDIT SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP 2
Full Name Professor Rachel Hiller
Organisation University College London & Anna Freud Centre
Email Address r.hiller@ucl.ac.uk
Role Mentor
Description of Support Provided
Professor Rachel Hiller leads the Child Trauma and Recovery Research Group at UCL as a professor of Child &
Adolescent Mental Health and is the head of postgraduate studies at the Anna Freud Centre. Professor Hilller’s
research focusses on the identification of key psychological and social processes linking trauma and maltreatment
experiences to mental health. Additionally, she has expertise on the development and testing of scalable mental
health intervention for trauma-exposed young people and the implementation within and across social care and
mental health services. Importantly, Professor Hiller is renowned for co-developing mixed-methods research
across sectors that have typically not been represented in research. Currently, professor Hiller leads multiple trials,
including the MRC-funded ADaPT trial and the NIHR-ARC-funded ReThink project. Importantly, she has extensive
experience in the supervision and mentoring of early career researchers.
During the placement, Professor Hiller will provide frequent one-to-one supervision meetings focussing on applied,
mixed methods, cross-sector research in the field of complex trauma. The various research group meetings she
chairs, or is involved in, will form great opportunities for gaining feedback on the fellowship proposal. Additionally,
access to the participation teams within Anna Freud will provide a valuable resource for co-development of future
projects.
/ EDIT SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP 3
Full Name Professor Carmine Pariante
Organisation King's College London
Email Address carmine.pariante@kcl.ac.uk
Role Mentor
Description of Support Provided
Professor Pariante is Professor of Biological Psychiatry at the Institute of Psychiatry, Psychology and
Neuroscience, KCL, and Consultant Perinatal Psychiatrist at the South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation
Trust. He has received multiple awards, including the 2017 Andrea Leadsom Award for Outstanding Contribution
to the Field of Infant Mental Health. Professor Pariante’s research focusses on the role of stress and inflammation
in the development of mental disorders and has a particular interest in the perinatal period. Currently, Professor
Pariante works on multiple projects, including the NIHR-funded Maudsley Biomedical Research Centre; the MRC-
funded CELEBRATE; and the Technology Strategy Board-funded HappyMums: understanding, predicting, and
treating depression in pregnancy to improve mothers and offspring mental health outcomes.
During the placement, Professor Pariante will provide frequent one-to-one supervision aiming to discuss clinical
and neurobiological aspects of the advanced fellowship proposal. In addition, professor Pariante’s expertise in
accessible dissemination of mental health research will aid in the development of future dissemination plans
through an in-depth introduction to his award-winning online magazine.
/ EDIT SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP 4
Full Name Professor Helen Sharp
Organisation Liverpool University
Email Address hmsharp@liverpool.ac.uk
Role Mentor
Description of Support Provided
Professor Helen Sharp leads the Perinatal, Infant and Child Mental Health research group in the Department of
Primary Care and Mental Health at Liverpool University. She is a practising Consultant Clinical Psychologist and
currently leads the MRC-funded longitudinal study ‘The Bangalore Child Health and Development Study’ which
aims to identify shared and distinctive risk and protective factors for child mental health, including early parenting,
in the UK and India. She is the mental health lead for 'Children Growing Up in Liverpool', focussing on identifying
mechanisms underlying inequalities of child development outcomes. Professor Sharp was the National Chair of
the Faculty of Perinatal Psychology, British Psychological Society for five years advising government, Heath
Education England and NHS England on policy and workforce development for perinatal mental health. She co-
authored the Royal College of Psychiatry 2018 (Framework for Routine Outcome Measures in Perinatal Psychiatry
CR216). She leads an active MRC GCRF programme grant with a team evaluating the predictive consequences of
perinatal mental health, early adversities and parenting quality for child outcomes across cultures
During the placement, Professor Sharp will provide frequent one-to-one supervision aiming to discuss the clinical,
equalities and parent-infant relational aspects of the fellowship proposal.

support_text.text:
Ruth O’Shaughnessy is a Consultant Clinical Psychologist and Co-Clinical Lead for the award-winning Cheshire and Merseyside Specialist Perinatal Service and for the North West Coast Perinatal Clinical Network. She has published numerous academic papers and book chapters in the field of attachment, perinatal, and parental mental illness. Importantly, she has substantial clinical and academic experience in the field of perinatal mental health inequalities, barriers to service access and treatment uptake as well as parenting across cultures, e.g. she co- authored the service-wide guidance on opening up conversations about race, ethnicity and culture. She also has extensive experience of supervising early to mid-career professionals. During the placement, Dr O’Shaughnessy will provide frequent one-to-one supervision meetings focussing on clinical and equalities aspects of the advanced fellowship proposal. Additionally, Dr O’Shaughnessy is ideally placed to introduce me to a wide range of professionals with extensive experience of culturally-sensitive and trauma-informed implementation of evidence-based therapies in NHS PMHS. The various research group meetings she chairs, or is involved in, will form great opportunities for gaining feedback on the fellowship proposal.
Professor Rachel Hiller leads the Child Trauma and Recovery Research Group at UCL as a professor of Child & Adolescent Mental Health and is the head of postgraduate studies at the Anna Freud Centre. Professor Hilller’s research focusses on the identification of key psychological and social processes linking trauma and maltreatment experiences to mental health. Additionally, she has expertise on the development and testing of scalable mental health intervention for trauma-exposed young people and the implementation within and across social care and mental health services. Importantly, Professor Hiller is renowned for co-developing mixed-methods research across sectors that have typically not been represented in research. Currently, professor Hiller leads multiple trials, including the MRC-funded ADaPT trial and the NIHR-ARC-funded ReThink project. Importantly, she has extensive experience in the supervision and mentoring of early career researchers. During the placement, Professor Hiller will provide frequent one-to-one supervision meetings focussing on applied, mixed methods, cross-sector research in the field of complex trauma. The various research group meetings she chairs, or is involved in, will form great opportunities for gaining feedback on the fellowship proposal. Additionally, access to the participation teams within Anna Freud will provide a valuable resource for co-development of future Reference: NIHR306150 Date submitted: 21/11/2024 Page 10 of 58 Development and Skills Enhancement Cohort 5 projects.
Professor Pariante is Professor of Biological Psychiatry at the Institute of Psychiatry, Psychology and Neuroscience, KCL, and Consultant Perinatal Psychiatrist at the South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trust. He has received multiple awards, including the 2017 Andrea Leadsom Award for Outstanding Contribution to the Field of Infant Mental Health. Professor Pariante’s research focusses on the role of stress and inflammation in the development of mental disorders and has a particular interest in the perinatal period. Currently, Professor Pariante works on multiple projects, including the NIHR-funded Maudsley Biomedical Research Centre; the MRC- funded CELEBRATE; and the Technology Strategy Board-funded HappyMums: understanding, predicting, and treating depression in pregnancy to improve mothers and offspring mental health outcomes. During the placement, Professor Pariante will provide frequent one-to-one supervision aiming to discuss clinical and neurobiological aspects of the advanced fellowship proposal. In addition, professor Pariante’s expertise in accessible dissemination of mental health research will aid in the development of future dissemination plans through an in-depth introduction to his award-winning online magazine.
Ruth O’Shaughnessy is a Consultant Clinical Psychologist and Co-Clinical Lead for the award-winning Cheshire
and Merseyside Specialist Perinatal Service and for the North West Coast Perinatal Clinical Network. She has
published numerous academic papers and book chapters in the field of attachment, perinatal, and parental mental
illness. Importantly, she has substantial clinical and academic experience in the field of perinatal mental health
inequalities, barriers to service access and treatment uptake as well as parenting across cultures, e.g. she co-
authored the service-wide guidance on opening up conversations about race, ethnicity and culture. She also has
extensive experience of supervising early to mid-career professionals.
During the placement, Dr O’Shaughnessy will provide frequent one-to-one supervision meetings focussing on
clinical and equalities aspects of the advanced fellowship proposal. Additionally, Dr O’Shaughnessy is ideally
placed to introduce me to a wide range of professionals with extensive experience of culturally-sensitive and
trauma-informed implementation of evidence-based therapies in NHS PMHS. The various research group
meetings she chairs, or is involved in, will form great opportunities for gaining feedback on the fellowship proposal.

/ EDIT SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP 4
Full Name Professor Helen Sharp
Organisation Liverpool University
Email Address hmsharp@liverpool.ac.uk
Role Mentor
Description of Support Provided
Professor Helen Sharp leads the Perinatal, Infant and Child Mental Health research group in the Department of
Primary Care and Mental Health at Liverpool University. She is a practising Consultant Clinical Psychologist and
currently leads the MRC-funded longitudinal study ‘The Bangalore Child Health and Development Study’ which
aims to identify shared and distinctive risk and protective factors for child mental health, including early parenting,
in the UK and India. She is the mental health lead for 'Children Growing Up in Liverpool', focussing on identifying
mechanisms underlying inequalities of child development outcomes. Professor Sharp was the National Chair of
the Faculty of Perinatal Psychology, British Psychological Society for five years advising government, Heath
Education England and NHS England on policy and workforce development for perinatal mental health. She co-
authored the Royal College of Psychiatry 2018 (Framework for Routine Outcome Measures in Perinatal Psychiatry
CR216). She leads an active MRC GCRF programme grant with a team evaluating the predictive consequences of
perinatal mental health, early adversities and parenting quality for child outcomes across cultures
During the placement, Professor Sharp will provide frequent one-to-one supervision aiming to discuss the clinical,
equalities and parent-infant relational aspects of the fellowship proposal.

a number of years now. Given her strong track record of impactful academic publications, securing funding and
overall academic success, a starting point of the lower band of a senior research fellow is deemed appropriate by
the Head of the Division of Psychology and Language Sciences at UCL.
The £3000 super visions costs are included within the 'Training and Development Cost'.
